summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Microsoft's existential crisis stems from having achieved their original mission of putting a computer on every desk, leaving them unprepared for the post-PC era that Steve Jobs predicted in 1996. He acknowledges his conflicted feelings about the shift away from general-purpose computing, noting that most people don't need machines that can do everything, and that the complexity baked into traditional operating systems makes them ill-suited for the modern world. The new iPad's revolutionary high-resolution display represents the kind of fundamental innovation that the general-purpose computing industry failed to deliver for over a decade. Atwood concludes that when simplified, fixed-function devices are not only 'enough' computer for most people but also drive genuine innovation, the post-PC era deserves to be welcomed.
key_points: Microsoft achieved their 'computer on every desk' mission years ago but never developed a vision for what comes next
The complexity inherent in general-purpose computing (updates, toolbars, settings, anti-virus, filesystems) makes PCs fundamentally incompatible with the post-PC world
Most people don't truly need a general-purpose computer—browsing, email, and basic tasks cover their needs
The iPad 3's high-resolution display represents a thirty-year breakthrough that the PC industry failed to deliver despite having the technology
Post-PC devices are now driving fundamental innovations in computing that general-purpose computers abandoned
money_quotes: When you wake up one day to discover that you've completely conquered the world… what comes next?
The PC wars are over. Done. Microsoft won a long time ago.
Apple is arguably the biggest (and in terms of financials, now literally the biggest) enemy of general purpose computing with the iPhone and iPad.
Doing potentially 'anything' comes at a steep cost in complexity.
All the stuff you hate when your Mom calls you for tech support? It's deeply embedded into of the culture and design of every single general purpose computer.
iPad 3 reviews that complain 'all they did was improve the display' are clueless bordering on stupidity.
Until the iPhone and iPad, near as I can tell, nobody else was even trying to improve resolution on computer displays.

At the point where these simple, fixed function Post-PC era computing devices are not just 'enough' computer for most folks, but also fundamentally innovating in computing as a whole… well, all I can say is bring on the post-PC era.
key_insight: The post-PC era arrived not because tablets and phones became good enough to replace computers, but because they started delivering fundamental innovations—like high-resolution displays—that the complacent PC industry had abandoned for years.
